verses.text: Joash begins his reign at seven and does right 'all the days of Jehoiada the priest.' The young king is steadied by a godly guide. A faithful mentor can hold a heart to God — but the test comes when that support is gone.

Joash begins his reign at seven and does right 'all the days of Jehoiada the priest.' The young king is steadied by a godly guide. A faithful mentor can hold a heart to God — but the test comes when that support is gone.

Joash begins his reign at seven and does right 'all the days of Jehoiada the priest.' The young king is steadied by a godly guide. A faithful mentor can hold a heart to God — but the test comes when that support is gone.

Joash resolves to repair the neglected temple, but the Levites are slow, for Athaliah's sons had broken it down and stripped it for Baal. Good intentions meet old ruin. The damage of past sin is real, and restoring God's house takes resolve to overcome delay.

Joash resolves to repair the neglected temple, but the Levites are slow, for Athaliah's sons had broken it down and stripped it for Baal. Good intentions meet old ruin. The damage of past sin is real, and restoring God's house takes resolve to overcome delay.

A chest is set at the gate, and the people bring their money so gladly that the collectors are kept busy. Willing hearts make a glad work. When God's people give freely, His house is repaired without compulsion or complaint.

A chest is set at the gate, and the people bring their money so gladly that the collectors are kept busy. Willing hearts make a glad work. When God's people give freely, His house is repaired without compulsion or complaint.

A chest is set at the gate, and the people bring their money so gladly that the collectors are kept busy. Willing hearts make a glad work. When God's people give freely, His house is repaired without compulsion or complaint.

A chest is set at the gate, and the people bring their money so gladly that the collectors are kept busy. Willing hearts make a glad work. When God's people give freely, His house is repaired without compulsion or complaint.

The funds restore the temple and furnish its vessels, and offerings continue all Jehoiada's days. The house of God flourishes again. Faithful giving, wisely used, keeps the worship of God alive for a generation.

The funds restore the temple and furnish its vessels, and offerings continue all Jehoiada's days. The house of God flourishes again. Faithful giving, wisely used, keeps the worship of God alive for a generation.

Jehoiada dies at a hundred and thirty and is buried among the kings, honoured for the good he did in Israel. A long life well spent earns lasting respect. God remembers those who serve Him faithfully, and so do His people.

Jehoiada dies at a hundred and thirty and is buried among the kings, honoured for the good he did in Israel. A long life well spent earns lasting respect. God remembers those who serve Him faithfully, and so do His people.

After Jehoiada's death the princes flatter Joash, and he forsakes the LORD's house for idols; wrath comes, yet God still sends prophets to call them back. The prop removed, the weak heart drifts. God's patience pleads even with those who abandon Him.

After Jehoiada's death the princes flatter Joash, and he forsakes the LORD's house for idols; wrath comes, yet God still sends prophets to call them back. The prop removed, the weak heart drifts. God's patience pleads even with those who abandon Him.

After Jehoiada's death the princes flatter Joash, and he forsakes the LORD's house for idols; wrath comes, yet God still sends prophets to call them back. The prop removed, the weak heart drifts. God's patience pleads even with those who abandon Him.

Zechariah, Jehoiada's son, rebukes them in God's name, and Joash has him stoned in the temple court, forgetting all his father's kindness; dying, he cries, 'The LORD look upon it, and require it.' Ingratitude hardens into murder. Innocent blood does not cry to God in vain.

Zechariah, Jehoiada's son, rebukes them in God's name, and Joash has him stoned in the temple court, forgetting all his father's kindness; dying, he cries, 'The LORD look upon it, and require it.' Ingratitude hardens into murder. Innocent blood does not cry to God in vain.

Zechariah, Jehoiada's son, rebukes them in God's name, and Joash has him stoned in the temple court, forgetting all his father's kindness; dying, he cries, 'The LORD look upon it, and require it.' Ingratitude hardens into murder. Innocent blood does not cry to God in vain.

Soon a small Syrian company defeats Judah's great army and executes judgment on Joash, 'because they had forsaken the LORD.' A handful overcomes a host when God withdraws. No numbers can save the people who have first cast off their God.

Soon a small Syrian company defeats Judah's great army and executes judgment on Joash, 'because they had forsaken the LORD.' A handful overcomes a host when God withdraws. No numbers can save the people who have first cast off their God.

Left badly wounded, Joash is slain on his bed by his own servants for the blood of Zechariah, and is not buried among the kings. The reign that began so bright ends in shame. A start with God means little if the heart turns from Him at the last.
